refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up / total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Validation via field trials or controlled test environment with simulated network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and trigger failsafe response within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant network paths (primary + backup cellular/radio) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log availability metrics.
DP-B: Deploy dual independent communication stacks (LTE + private radio) with load-balancing and seamless handover; implement real-time link quality monitoring.
DP-C: Use a single primary network path with periodic health checks and manual recovery procedures; maintain availability logs.
DP-D: Implement multi-layer redundancy (network + transport + application) with predictive link quality estimation and proactive rerouting.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Deploy dual independent communication stacks (LTE + private radio) with load-balancing and seamless handover; implement real-time link quality monitoring and automatic failover to achieve ≥0.999 availability.

potential_sources: DP-B couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection (LTE + private radio availability at site).
DP-B couples to vehicle hardware (dual modem capability, antenna placement).
DP-B couples to tele-operator interface (must support seamless handover without operator intervention).
DP-B couples to Safety Services (failsafe response on link loss must be coordinated).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable because they reflect real system dependencies. DP-B is superior to DP-C (single path insufficient for 0.999 target) and more practical than DP-D (multi-layer redundancy adds complexity without proportional benefit for this requirement). DP-A is similar but DP-B's explicit load-balancing and seamless handover better address availability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: LTE coverage is available at customer sites; if not, private radio alone may not achieve 0.999.
Assumption: Vehicle hardware supports dual modems and antenna diversity.
Assumption: Tele-operator training includes seamless handover scenarios.
Risk: Handover latency during failover could exceed 100 ms if not carefully tuned.
Risk: Link quality estimation algorithms may be inaccurate in poor RF environments.
Risk: Cost of dual communication stacks may exceed customer budget.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to explicitly specify: (1) target handover latency ≤ 50 ms, (2) link quality thresholds for proactive switching, (3) fallback behavior if both paths fail (e.g., safe stop), (4) cost/benefit trade-off analysis vs. DP-A.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual communication stack (LTE + private radio), link health monitoring, failover logic, and availability metrics logging. Interfaces with Vehicle (for modem hardware and antenna control), Safety Services (for failsafe coordination), and external Network Link (for actual connectivity).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Tele-operator commands (steering, throttle, bucket control, etc.) flow inbound to Teleoperation module via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics) flows outbound from Teleoperation module via Telemetry interface to tele-operator display.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives SafetyAlert signals (e.g., link loss detected) and sends failsafe commands (e.g., safe stop) to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module manages dual network paths (LTE + private radio) via Network Link interface; monitors link state and triggers failover.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, dual stack management, and failover. Minimal diffusion: only hardware modem control (5%) leaks to Vehicle module.

Vehicle module owns physical hardware (modems, antennas) but not the communication logic. Assigning DP-B to Vehicle would scatter link management across multiple modules (Vehicle + Teleoperation + Safety Services), creating high diffusion and coupling.

Safety Services owns failsafe response (e.g., safe stop on link loss) but not the link availability mechanism itself. Assigning DP-B to Safety Services would couple safety logic to network management, violating separation of concerns.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-B here would bypass architectural layering and create diffusion across all sub-modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns the dual communication stack, link health monitoring, failover logic, and availability metrics. Vehicle module provides hardware modem/antenna interfaces. Safety Services receives link-loss alerts and executes failsafe response. This assignment achieves high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05).
containment_risks: Risk: If vehicle hardware (modem, antenna) is not properly abstracted, Teleoperation may become tightly coupled to specific hardware variants.
Risk: Failover logic must be coordinated with Safety Services; if SafetyAlert interface is not well-defined, link loss response may be delayed or inconsistent.
Risk: Network Link interface must provide clean abstraction of dual paths; if underlying network infrastructure is unreliable, Teleoperation cannot achieve 0.999 target regardless of software design.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define clear hardware abstraction layer (HAL) in Vehicle module for modem/antenna control. Specify SafetyAlert interface contract: link-loss detection → failsafe command latency ≤ 100 ms. Validate Network Link interface assumptions with network infrastructure team.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual independent communication stacks with load-balancing and seamless handover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and technically feasible. Redundancy and failover are proven approaches for high-availability systems. No independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Modem/antenna hardware control (5% diffusion to Vehicle module) is acceptable and expected.
Failsafe coordination with Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) is a necessary coupling, not diffusion, because it reflects a real system dependency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. The 5% diffusion (hardware control) is minimal and well-justified. No alternative module assignment yields better containment without introducing higher coupling or violating separation of concerns.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is straightforward: continuous link state monitoring during evaluation period. Acceptance criteria (≥0.999) is quantitative and testable. Field trials or controlled test environment can validate.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Teleoperation module is clearly responsible for link availability. Vehicle provides hardware. Safety Services provides failsafe response. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and traceable to DP-B.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify handover latency target (≤50 ms) and link quality thresholds.
Define Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle for modem/antenna control.
Specify SafetyAlert interface SLA: link-loss detection → failsafe command ≤100 ms.
Validate Network Link interface assumptions with network infrastructure team (LTE + private radio availability at customer sites).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, failsafe response, metrics logging). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. DP-B is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module, but sub-level design decisions (e.g., specific failover algorithm, link quality estimation method, metrics schema) must be addressed at the next decomposition level. Safety criticality (high) requires rigorous sub-level analysis of failsafe response timing and coordination with Safety Services.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with ≥0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with ≥0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and trigger failsafe response within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics (uptime, downtime, failover events, link quality) for post-evaluation analysis.

assumptions: LTE coverage is available at customer sites; if not, private radio alone may not achieve 0.999.
Vehicle hardware supports dual modems and antenna diversity.
Tele-operator training includes seamless handover scenarios.
Network Link interface provides clean abstraction of dual paths.
SafetyAlert interface latency is ≤100 ms for failsafe response.
open_risks: Handover latency during failover could exceed 100 ms if not carefully tuned.
Link quality estimation algorithms may be inaccurate in poor RF environments.
Cost of dual communication stacks may exceed customer budget.
Hardware abstraction layer (HAL) between Teleoperation and Vehicle must be well-defined to avoid tight coupling.
Failsafe response coordination with Safety Services must be validated for timing and consistency.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure assessment: LTE + private radio coverage at customer sites.
Vehicle hardware specification: dual modem capability, antenna placement, power budget.
Failover algorithm design and simulation: target handover latency ≤50 ms.
Link quality estimation method: validation in representative RF environments.
SafetyAlert interface contract: link-loss detection → failsafe command latency ≤100 ms.
Cost/benefit analysis: dual communication stacks vs. customer budget.
Field trial plan: evaluation period, measurement methodology, acceptance criteria.
